title: Add post-merge graph repair for large accumulated runs
problem: When Quiver scales from small pilots to dozens or hundreds of papers, per-paper serial deltas will not be enough to guarantee a clean global graph. The merged graph can retain incomplete concept consolidation, semantically duplicated nodes, stale ontology-status conflicts, missing support edges, dangling review frontier, and dependency cycles introduced across papers. These problems may not be visible in a single-paper validation pass.
proposed_change: Add a dedicated post-merge repair and review stage after accumulated runs. It should analyze the normalized graph globally, detect cycles and strongly connected components, surface probable duplicate or under-merged concepts, identify incomplete dependency neighborhoods, and produce a reviewable repair plan rather than silently rewriting the graph. Repairs should be recorded as explicit review/patch deltas so the process stays auditable.
acceptance_criteria: On a larger multi-paper test project, quiver can produce a post-merge audit that reports dependency cycles, suspicious SCCs, duplicate/near-duplicate concept clusters, ontology-status conflicts, and incomplete frontier neighborhoods; accepted repairs are persisted as explicit graph patch artifacts and the rebuilt graph has no unreviewed cycles in dependency_depends_on-style relations.

observations.context: After ag95-diverse-002: 8 papers produced 213 nodes and 342 edges with low cross-paper reuse, 48 frontier nodes, 13 near-duplicate candidates, and one ontology-status conflict; user noted larger runs will need explicit merge repair and cycle handling.

ag95-diverse-003-16p completed with 16 papers, 434 nodes, 740 edges, 6 multi-paper reused nodes, 6 ontology-status conflicts, 64 frontier nodes, and no SCC cycles or self-edges in the normalized graph. This run did not require immediate repair, but it confirms post-merge repair should target ontology-status conflicts and duplicate/similar concepts as scale increases.

history.note: Accepted as a known scale-up risk before larger supervised batches.
